item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my absolute favorite SCPs. There's just enough there to piece together the narrative, and it's a good narrative. And it easily has one of the best first person descents into madness I've seen in any horror story, and I've seen a lot of those.

Some possibly spoilery thoughts:

+ show block
– hide block

I interpret the light as destroying the metaphysical "anchor" that keeps something squarely in a single reality, making it exist in multiple parallel realities at once, maybe even every possible reality at once. That's why the researcher says they're not ready for "what's out there"; every possible reality in the Foundation's carefully monitored test chambers is much easier to deal with than the chaos of the world outside. This idea fits in with Star Signals, which describes the Fifth World as your ideal version of reality, the universe sculpted by you into your perfect shape. By severing your connection to one single world, the light gives you the freedom to move to any world. But the human mind isn't made for this, and very strange things happen to a person when they do this.

Most assume the 'body' referred to in "Don't worry about the body" must be a corpse, but I think that line should actually be taken as a much more general statement. As in, don't worry about the physical body, the mind is what matters. Given 3005's general effects on human bodies, this seems like solid advice.

Honestly, I love this SCP, along with 1425, so much that I have trouble accepting any other take on the Fifth Church into my personal headcanon, which I suppose is ironic given the subject material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This comment made by DisquietRiot on SCP-1523's discussion thread got me to realize something about SCP-3005 that I don't think anyone else has mentioned.
To quote DisquietRiot:

the idea of people expelling or ejecting their souls as a method of self-improvement is, to me at least, very creepy and something I think later Fifthist entries dropped unnecessarily

I agree, though I don't think it was entirely dropped in later entires. Also, removing the soul isn't the only thing that happens. :)

Some things to consider in SCP-3005:

+ I don't think this is too long but I put it in a collapsible anyway
- Close

I asked him to deal with the body and he said “don't worry about the body”. I'm surprised, because she wasn't flung apart and I haven't seen anyone else around here in a long while. Who deals with the bodies? Is anyone cleaning the test chamber?

Who is cleaning the test chamber? That is the question, or maybe it's the wrong question to ask. After all, you don't need a solution to a problem that solves itself.

There are no more test subjects and nobody else. There is only one way in. The booth needs to be operated, but I have a special plan.

Someone needs to operate the booth to let other people in, but theres only one person left. You'd think you need at least one other person to operate the booth. But you don't. You just need somebody to operate the booth.

Don't worry about the body.

Don't worry about the body.

Input: dont
Distance: worri
Time: abot
Output: the
Comments: bodee

I guess bodies aren't very good at spelling.

- Close

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very late but reading this explanation for the second or third time and thought about how "anchor" and "archon" are anagrams, and it's kind of fucking me up.

E: Actually, this is mentioned on later pages and I'm certain I read those before. But I forgot that I read them until now, so I got to blow my own mind with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably Keter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+One (5) Upvote(s)

Pretty damn good - the descent into madness is done in a very different way to the out-of-character mindfuckery of 3999, in spite of the comparisons. In terms of a fridge horror-y "you don't know what it is, or what it does, all you know is what it did to us", this is certainly a worthy spiritual successor to 579; the punchline doesn't hit as hard as In the event of an unsuccessful Action 10-Israfil-B, no further action will be necessary, but the buildup is more than good enough to make up for it.

As for the whole broken anchor, SRA-imposing-what-it-thinks-is-reality idea, is that at all linked to this? Because while it's not my headcanon about them, I love the idea that this isn't any outside anomaly, that it's just what happens to SRAs far enough past their use-by-date, and that the Foundation's full of ticking time bombs.
Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FIVE (5) FIVE (5) FIVE (5) FIVE (5)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. A█████?

Anyway, +1. Descent-into-madness is a trope but this is one of the best-executed examples on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creative, different +1. Really draws you in until the last point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Daheck is this. And why is it creepy and good.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know when I imagine this, the shade of pink I see in my head is like an afterimage, like when you look at a light and look away or turn the light off, you still percieve a color, I usually kinda see it as this cyan-pink, a color I would not really know how to best describe it but I am sure you probably know what I am talking about.

Images in your head from light that has ceased to be.

sometimes in those optical illusions they have you stare into an empty space.

You look at an image and then look at the empty shape and your mind places the image in the emptyness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pink is NOT light red. Pink is violet and red mixed together. Colors and color perception are weird.

Edit: the majority of people can detect 3 colors. Colorblind people, depending on the colorblindness, can usually only see two. A VERY tiny minority of people can see four. The actual world they see is totally alien to the rest of us, but it generally goes unnoticed because who the hell goes to a doctor for seeing colors weirdly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait….. there can't be pink light?

My goodness, I know nothing after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. Is the "pink light" a reference to how light can't be pink (pink isn't a wavelength of light?) seems like it'll fit in with other fifthist stuff.

item.content:encoded: That reminds me, what would Mr.Eaten be?

item.title: Re: ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
item.content:encoded: I feel the same way. Well-written as it was, I didn't have any emotional response to this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, what are those little pin numbers next to the D classes, e.g (6-95-241), (6-95-278), (6-95-222)? Is it a personal way of tagging them other tahn just one large number or…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … why are people complaining about it being "old hat"? Yes, it makes use of a narrative structure that's been around since (at the very least) Resident Evil gave us "itchy, tasty". So? This is an "itchy, tasty" log but it's a Foundation researcher going completely bonkers because of the thing that's only safe on land and twisting causality itself into a pretzel in the process. Hell, it even makes use of what I'm convinced is an allusion to trepanning, which frankly is fantastic. And of course on top of that you have the veneer of what makes SCP great: using clinical detached tone to describe objectively abhorrent shit like "I cleaned up her fingers". Gorgeous work, and the explanation of what the blackboxed six-letter word entails just made it even better.

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded: Very amazing and complex I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which we all know is a major influence of Sarkicism:

Yes, I never said that Sarkicists don't have archons.

Huh. Maybe I haven't read enough Fifthist scips, but the actual theology of the Fifthist Church seems so elusive.

There isn't one, it is para-religious, rather than theological.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fifthists don't have a theology. Fifthists have inane ramblings about stars that make zero sense. They're also completely right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Archons are a concept from Gnosticism.

Which we all know is a major influence of Sarkicism:

"Blind" and driven solely by instinct, Yaldabaoth is depicted as being accompanied by six otherworldly entities known as "Archons"
— Sarkic Hub

Love the archons. When you hate them, they see you.
— Star Signals, Ch.9 Section 3

Huh. Maybe I haven't read enough Fifthist scips, but the actual theology of the Fifthist Church seems so elusive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Archons are a concept from Gnosticism. They definitely exist in fifthism:

Love the archons. When you hate them, they see you.
— Star Signals, Ch.9 Section 3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you're thinking of Sarkicism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this article had a stroke.

Good luck suppressing the color pink, I guess?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fifthism has "archons", right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I mull an article over before voting, but this was an instant +1. It's the no-idea-what's-going-on style, which either hits or misses. The ones that miss frustrate you because your imagination doesn't get engaged, so you just want to know what's happening. Successful ones like this tease you enough that you enjoy the journey of discovery. It's a playground for the imagination, forged with proper pacing, tension, intrigue and mystery. This article nails it.

I hope to see more from you in the future. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I check in on it, a party game stares up at me.

This is a minor example of how well-crafted writing improves everything, including a generic test log. I pick this line not because it blew me away, but because it was the point where I realized consciously that I was enjoying reading the test logs more than I usually enjoy reading test logs. And this was before the real trippy stuff showed up. (well from my pov at least!)

This is very good — very evocative. I really liked the choice to just cut out most of the containment & description — really increased the feeling of helpless bafflement while also being very intriguing.

I did want more. I can't decide if the SCP itself needs more, or if I just want more because it works so well.

I will say that reading the explanation made this significantly more awesome for me. I'll continue under a collapsible —

+ show block
– hide block

I understood the references to SRAs and liked them quite a bit. I really liked the censored word of 'anchor' (and that line was just seriously excellent anyway.) But knowing this is an SRA itself in contact with an anomaly throwing it seriously off makes me really love the piece. I think that making it easier for an informed reader to guess that secret would make the SCP even better, although admittedly that would still be lost on most of the audience. So consider that FWIW.

Anyway, this is great, upvoted :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm… Fifthism is hit or miss for me, but tends towards the latter. I didn't feel scared when reading it at all, not even intrigued, just confused and trying to figure out if there was anything deeper I was missing.

OK, so Fifthism is responsible here, and generally causes things that are indescribably nonsensical. I caught on to the 5s everywhere, I get that. But that alone does nothing for me. "The article descends into illegibility" has been done many, many times, and usually has something else behind it to back it up. This one doesn't, as far as I could tell. It fails to scare me the same way 1981 fails with its word salad, but at least that one has some semblance of narrative with the prediction angle.

I Am At The Centre of Everything That Happens To Me did the whole 'breakdown of reality into nonsense that's plain wrong' angle better, IMO.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's well inline with Fifthist information that it has antimemetic effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where is "archon" used in this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Archon is a word: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… "archon" is an anagram of "anchor". Is that deliberate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this was one of those where I went back and forth:

  • The idea that this version is recovered from corrupted data: +1
  • The writing of the comments read more like load screen flavor text than a Foundation document: -1
  • Output: One Religion: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is easily my favorite post for the 3000 contest. The style of writing is great and gives the entire thing a creepy vibe. This is easily the creepiest entry, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it, why does he craved for the pink light?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It must be me, but I don't 'get' the love for this.

It's an undefinable. And not even an undefinable where they tried, HARD, to define it, like 055. It's undefinable, it's loose. We don't know how to locate it, contain it once we do, or hang onto it afterwards. The last time we tried to do anything to it, it drove someone BUGSHIT nuts. And possibly did messed up stuff to a bunch of test subjects. Can't be sure, because the guy who recorded it was nuts. I'm not even sure where the horror is, other than in the "If I worked for the Foundation, having this thing loose would be bad," sort of way.

I'll dig more into the Fifthists, and see if that changes my mind about this one. But right now, it's a great view of a descent into madness… and nothing else, for me. Sorry, downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is now one of my favorite entries right up there with Anantashesha.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am embarrassed by how long it took for me to realize this thing, whatever it was, was a Fifthist thing. (I figured it out exactly when the SCP wanted me to, for the record, which was at the very end.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah don't get it

I know that it might just be 'don't worry about the body' but no it can't just be that

please don't tell me it's just that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Input: dont
Distance: worri
Time: abot
Output: the
Comments: bodee

What the fuck is this?

I come back after a few days to see this, which I know wasn't there before, and ho-lee-shit, creep factor times five ten!

(Also, given the spoilers above, I guess I oughta delete 2832, you've done the idea considerably better.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait. Wait a second.

I need to tell a director or an overseer or anyone who can tell everybody to stop using those ███████. All of you. They're chained and lobotomized …

I am now headcanoning a connection to my PI article. Please never tell me if I'm wrong :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For those who are curious enough about what's going on here to seek an explanation, I'll go ahead and lay it all out.

Input: Spoilers
Output: 1 (ONE) religion

No, the 5s aren't a red herring. This is Fifthism rearing up, as it tends to do, unconnected to any other instances of the faith. There are lots of little references here, mostly to 1425 — the title itself is a nod to the back cover material from Star Signals.

This trails back to the central mechanics of the Fifth Church, which I've discussed before with Famine, Kaktus, qntm, and other authors who have dug into the GOI. There is no "god of Fifthism", there is no particular object of worship (although certain stars may be given titles, like 'saint' or 'archon'), but there is an entity at the center of it: some higher-dimensional being whose passage through our spacetime has caused certain gashes and blisters which form this infohazard and give it the influence it has over reality. It might be a product of this other world — the "Fifth World" — but it could also simply be a result of two incompatible planes of existence mingling together. It could be that that other universe doesn't exist at all and it's a sort of multiversal glitch, the Minus World that manifests in rifts between "where" and "nowhere". Hence the deep confusion going on in the mind of this researcher exposed to it.

Next we have the blackboxed word that the researcher writes — something that the Foundation didn't consider clear for general consumption. And that's "anchor". The current Foundation has a dependence on Scranton Reality Anchors all over the place, a black-box solution similar to what telekill used to be. I won't get too deep into my headcanon on SRAs — I intend to write more about it sometime — but in my view they're simply a form of counter-reality-bending. These massive machines bend reality, but they do so with a preset in place, what the Foundation considers the mundane world.

But what happens when an SRA becomes confused? What happens when a reality anchor encounters a deeply strange anomaly that the Foundation doesn't fully understand, one that leaves it confused as to which world it's in, which reality it's supposed to maintain, or whether it's still in a universe at all? What does it seek to maintain when it's lost all grip on reality?

That's what we're here to watch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good catch.

To quote Borges: "In a riddle whose answer is 'chess', what is the one word that cannot be used?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Magnificently done.

item.title: New ending
item.content:encoded: I went ahead and edited the ending a bit. Hopefully it's stronger for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably my new favorite entry. Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a triumphant return, Silber. Fantastic stuff, and extremely unique.

item.title: Tips
item.content:encoded: Can you give me tips on how to make an SCP pop, HORROR

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some nods in the general direction of Constellation Starfish, but mostly it involves a lot of references to 1425. For example, the first sentence of the back-cover summary of 1425 is pretty significant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured it was a fit hist thing, I just don't think I understand the fithism stuff enough to parse this!

Which is the danger with all these quasi-canons, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was at +55 when I opened it and I felt like if I upvoted it I was gonna get cursed. Upvoted anyway, if I'm flung apart I will downvote with my last breath.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite part here is the breakdown in causality, with things happening before they start. The betamax thing is a nice touch, too: When betamax camcorders start showing up outside of the early 80's, you're not in Kansas anymore, Toto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Five (5) etc ending wasn't powerful enough to get me to upvote, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is very Fallen London. And Fallen London is great, so that counts for something. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The most Lovecraftian entry I've seen so far - better, in many respects, because it shows the progression into madness due to the eldritch thing that the audience should not know about, rather than just telling us that it sends people mad.

Writing convincing Fifthist logic is difficult (especially when you're using it to tell a story without sacrificing character in the name of exposition), and this does a lovely job of juxtaposing the confused thinking required with the banality of the Foundation's approach to horrors. "Output: Viscera" and then "But I have a special plan" - the combination of those two produced a real frisson.

item.title: Re: ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
item.content:encoded: Pretty much my response. It's interesting as a study in the narrative effects of expungement, but I didn't get much out of it. The total elision of the central idea wasn't really a problem, it just seemed like what was left a collection of well-executed but also well-trodden tropes and that couldn't quite engage me.
